sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=36, col=48).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 50, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'Moves green pixel right 2px within container (VERIFIED step 3)', 'LEFT': 'Moves green pixel left 2px within container (VERIFIED step 4)', 'UP': 'Moves green up within container if space exists (VERIFIED). No portal swap ever occurred - previous claim was wrong.', 'DOWN': 'Moves green down within container if space exists (VERIFIED)', 'MOUSE': 'Clicking on green pixel causes HUD timer bar to shrink by 1 pixel (VERIFIED step 42). Second click has no additional effect.'}
- Object taxonomy: {'green_pixel': 'Active 2x2 green (N) object, controlled by arrow keys', 'divider_blocks': 'White 2x2 blocks at col 32-33, vertical line', 'colored_pixels': '2x2 objects in left containers: yellow, blue, red, dark red, green', 'hud_timer': 'White bar at row 63, shrinks when MOUSE clicks green pixel'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_bar': 'White horizontal bar at row 63, shrinks as timer/progress indicator. Decreases by 1 pixel per MOUSE click on green.', 'divider': 'Vertical line of white 2x2 blocks at col 32-33'}
Current level:
- World model: Two-panel layout. Left (charcoal bg) has gray containers with colored 2x2 pixels. Right (black bg) has white containers with red/blue shapes. Green pixel is the active object, moves within bottom container (rows 38-43, cols 8-13). MOUSE click on green shrinks the timer bar by 1. The timer bar started at ~44 pixels and is now ~43. This suggests the game is about clicking green a certain number of times, or clicking green after positioning it in specific locations.
- Goal model: The timer bar shrinks when clicking green. Goal may be to shrink it to zero, or to click green at specific positions to trigger color transfers to the right side. Need to explore whether clicking green at different positions or clicking other colored pixels has different effects.
- Recent findings: ['MOUSE on green at (42,12) caused board_changed=True (step 42)', 'Change was only in HUD timer bar: white bar at row 63 shrunk by 1 pixel', 'Second MOUSE click on green had no effect (step 43)', 'No arrow key movement has ever caused board_changed=True']
- Open questions: ['Does clicking green at different positions have different effects?', 'Does clicking other colored pixels (yellow, blue, red) do anything?', 'Is the goal to shrink the timer to zero, or to trigger color transfers?', 'Do I need to move green to different containers before clicking?']
- Plan: Try MOUSE on other colored pixels (yellow, blue, red) to see if they have different effects. Also try moving green to different positions and clicking there.
- Strategy log: ['Abandoned portal swap theory: no action ever caused board_changed until MOUSE on green (step 42)', 'Movement actions (UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT) never cause board changes - only MOUSE does']
- Failed probes: ['MOUSE(20,24) on yellow pixel: no selection change (step 9)', 'UP at cols 8-9 rows 38-39: no change (step 16)', 'UP at cols 10-11 rows 38-39: no change (step 17)', 'RIGHT at cols 12-13 rows 38-39: no movement (step 13)', 'UP at cols 12-13 rows 38-39: no change (step 12)', 'UP at cols 12-13 rows 38-39: no move (step 37)', 'RIGHT from cols 12-13 rows 42-43: no move', 'MOUSE on green second time: no additional effect (step 43)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 49 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking the blue L-shape also worked - timer went from 39 to 38. So both right-side shapes can be clicked to shrink the timer by 1 each.
Now I see from the image that the left side has changed - the blue 4x4 object has moved up (it's now at the top container position), and the green pixel has also moved up. This is interesting - it seems like clicking the blue L-shape on the right caused the blue object on the left to move.
Wait, looking more carefully at the image, I can see:
- The blue 4x4 object is now in the top container (where yellow was)
- The yellow is still there
- The green pixel seems to be in a different position
Actually, let me look at the image more carefully. The left side now shows:
- Top container: blue 4x4 and yellow 2x2
- Middle container: red 4x6 and dark red 4x4
- Bottom container: gray with green 2x2
But wait, the blue 4x4 was already in the top container before. Let me check the segmentation to understand what changed.
Actually, looking at the image, it seems like the blue 4x4 on the left has moved to a different position within its container. Or maybe the green pixel moved.
Let me focus on what I know:
1. Timer is now 38
2. Both right-side shapes have been clicked
3. The game is not done yet
I need to figure out what to do next. The timer is at 38, and I need to reduce it further. I've already clicked:
- Green 2x2: -1
- Yellow 2x2: -1
- Blue 4x4: -2
- Dark red 4x4: -1
- Red T-shape: -1 (plus removed containers)
- Blue L-shape: -1
Total: -7 clicks so far that affected the timer.
But I can only click each object once. Let me check if there are other objects I haven't clicked yet. Looking at the left side, I haven't clicked the red 4x6 object (which didn't work before).
Wait, let me re-examine. The red 4x6 on the left didn't work before. But maybe I need to click it after clicking the right-side shapes?
Actually, let me look at the current image more carefully. The left side shows:
- Top: blue 4x4 and yellow 2x2
- Middle: red 4x6 and dark red 4x4
- Bottom: gray container with green 2x2
But I see the blue 4x4 seems to have moved - it's now overlapping with the red 4x6. Let me check the segmentation to understand what happened.
Actually, I think the key insight is that clicking right-side shapes causes left-side objects to move or change. Let me inspect the current state more carefully.
For now, let me try clicking the red 4x6 on the left again to see if it works now.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Blue L-shape click: timer 39->38
# Let me check what changed on the left side

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Left side colored objects:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('Y', 'b', 'R', 'r', 'N') and n['pixels'] > 1:
        print(f"  {n['color']}: {n['pixels']}px at {n['boundary'][:2]}")

# Check if red 4x6 still exists
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'R' and n['pixels'] == 24:
        print(f"\nRed 4x6 found at {n['boundary'][:2]}")

# Try clicking red 4x6 again (was at row 24-29, col 18-23)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 26, 'col': 20}])
print(f"\nRed 4x6 click: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed', False)}, done={last_action_result.get('done', False)}")

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] > 20:
        print(f"Timer: {n['pixels']}px")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "Moves green right 2px", "LEFT": "Moves green left 2px", "UP": "Moves green up if space", "DOWN": "Moves green down if space", "MOUSE": "Clicking colored objects shrinks timer. Left squares: 1-2px. Right shapes: 1px each, also removed containers."}, "object_taxonomy": {"green_pixel": "Active 2x2 green object", "divider_blocks": "White 2x2 at col 32-33", "colored_pixels": "Various colored objects in left containers", "right_shapes": "Red T-shape and blue L-shape on right"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "White bar row 63, timer"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Timer at 38. Both right-side shapes clicked. Left-side objects may have shifted.", "goal_model": "Reduce timer to 0 by clicking colored objects", "recent_findings": ["Red T-shape click: removed containers, timer -1", "Blue L-shape click: timer -1"], "open_questions": ["Can I click red 4x6 now?", "What other objects can I click?"], "current_plan": "Try clicking red 4x6 again, then find other clickable objects", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["Second click on same pixel: no effect", "Click on red 4x6 before: no effect"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
